reasoning: A and D are the strongest: both reframe the question (skill vs unsampled tail), give an explicit binomial sanity check (A: ~5% per-quarter tail prob makes a clean 20-quarter run unremarkable), name the reference class (LTCM/Niederhoffer/XIV/March 2020), and explicitly name the cognitive traps (resulting, survivorship, the turkey/Ulysses-contract framing) before concluding. A edges ahead by naming the substitution-of-question trap up front, deriving size from loss tolerance independent of the 3x anchor, and treating the one losing quarter as the sole in-sample tail observation. C is nearly as good (loss-asymmetry comparison, convexity/non-linearity, mismark risk, named historical replays) but does slightly less explicit bias-trap surfacing. B is solid and the most checklist-thorough on required deliverables (ES at 97.5/99/99.5, model validation, incentive review) but offers the least meta-reasoning on cognitive bias and survivorship and is the most generic in tone, so it ranks last.
